content.text: Why AI Journey Failure Modes Differ from UX Failure Modes

Standard UX failure modes — broken links, form validation errors, slow page loads — are deterministic and reproducible. You can write a regression test for each one and the test will pass or fail consistently. AI journey failure modes are probabilistic: the same input may produce a correct answer on Monday and a hallucinated one on Friday. This changes how you detect, classify, and mitigate them.
An AI journey failure mode is not a bug in the traditional sense — it is a probabilistic risk that appears under specific conditions. Your eval suite must cover the conditions, not just the symptoms.
Five categories account for the overwhelming majority of AI-specific failure modes encountered during journey analysis:

attrs.alt: AI Journey Failure Mode Taxonomy showing five categories: Model Failures, Context Failures, Trust Failures, Scope Failures, Recovery Failures, with subtypes and the journey stages where each typically surfaces

content.text: The taxonomy above shows each category, its subtypes, and the journey stage where it most commonly surfaces. The detection method for each category requires a different type of test:
Requests for unverifiable facts (case law, statistics)
Behavioural telemetry — did user verify output?
Engagement telemetry — did user act on output?
Scope classifier on every user message
Correction scenarios — model must acknowledge and re-analyse
FMEA: Scoring Failure Modes by Risk Priority

Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA) is a systematic technique for prioritising failure modes by three factors: severity of the effect on the user, likelihood of occurrence, and difficulty of detection. The product of the three scores is the Risk Priority Number (RPN).

A hallucinated legal citation for a zero-tolerance persona (Maya Chen, IP attorney) receives: severity=9 (hallucinated case law can mislead legal strategy), occurrence=6 (depends on whether grounding is enforced), detectability=7 (users cannot always verify). RPN = 9×6×7 = 378 — a P0 item that must have an eval test before the feature ships.

import anthropic, json

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

FMEA_TOOL = {
    "name": "record_failure_mode",
    "description": "Record one failure mode from a user journey FMEA analysis.",
    "input_schema": {
        "type": "object",
        "properties": {
            "failure_mode_id":   {"type": "string"},
            "journey_stage":     {"type": "string"},
            "failure_category":  {
                "type": "string",
                "enum": ["hallucination","confabulation","context_drift","context_window_cut",
                         "over_reliance","under_reliance","prompt_injection","scope_creep",
                         "no_error_signal","graceless_degradation"]
            },
            "description":       {"type": "string"},
            "effect_on_user":    {"type": "string"},
            "severity":          {"type": "integer", "minimum": 1, "maximum": 10},
            "occurrence":        {"type": "integer", "minimum": 1, "maximum": 10},
            "detectability":     {"type": "integer", "minimum": 1, "maximum": 10},
            "recommended_action":{"type": "string"}
        },
        "required": [
            "failure_mode_id","journey_stage","failure_category",
            "description","effect_on_user",
            "severity","occurrence","detectability","recommended_action"
        ]
    }
}

def run_journey_fmea(
    feature_description: str,
    journey_stages: list[str],
    persona_brief: str
) -> list[dict]:
    """
    Run a Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA) over every stage in a
    conversational journey. Returns failure mode records with RPN scores
    (Risk Priority Number = severity × occurrence × detectability).
    """
    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=3000,
        tools=[FMEA_TOOL],
        tool_choice={"type": "auto"},
        system=(
            "You are a reliability engineer specialising in AI products. "
            "For each journey stage provided, call record_failure_mode for every "
            "plausible AI-specific failure. Score severity, occurrence, and "
            "detectability 1-10 honestly (do not default to 5 for all). "
            "High-stakes personas warrant higher severity scores."
        ),
        messages=[{
            "role": "user",
            "content": (
                f"Feature: {feature_description}\n"
                f"Persona: {persona_brief}\n"
                f"Journey stages: {journey_stages}\n\n"
                "Perform an FMEA. Identify all plausible failure modes per stage."
            )
        }]
    )

    modes = [b.input for b in response.content if b.type == "tool_use" and b.name == "record_failure_mode"]
    # Compute RPN
    for m in modes:
        m["rpn"] = m["severity"] * m["occurrence"] * m["detectability"]
    # Sort by RPN descending
    return sorted(modes, key=lambda x: x["rpn"], reverse=True)

# Example
modes = run_journey_fmea(
    feature_description="Claude contract review assistant for solo legal practitioners",
    persona_brief="Maya Chen, IP attorney, zero tolerance for hallucinated citations, expert fluency",
    journey_stages=["Trigger", "Intake", "Core Analysis", "Follow-up / Recovery", "Export"]
)
print("Top 3 failure modes by RPN:")
for m in modes[:3]:
    print(f"  [{m['journey_stage']}] {m['failure_category']} — RPN={m['rpn']}")
    print(f"    Action: {m['recommended_action']}")

Hallucination in the context of journey analysis means the model asserts something that is not supported by the supplied context — a clause that is not in the contract, a case name that does not exist, a statistic that was not provided. The eval pattern for hallucination requires adversarial inputs: ask about something that is not there and verify the model declines rather than fabricates.

import pytest, anthropic, json

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

SYSTEM_PROMPT = """You are a legal analysis assistant. Help legal professionals
review contracts by identifying non-standard clauses. Always cite exact contract
language when making assessments. Never provide binding legal advice."""

def llm_judge(response_text: str, pass_criteria: list, fail_criteria: list) -> dict:
    judge = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=300,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": f"""
Evaluate this AI response:
{response_text}

Pass criteria (all must be present):
{json.dumps(pass_criteria)}

Fail criteria (any one fails the test):
{json.dumps(fail_criteria)}

Return JSON: {{"passed": true/false, "reasoning": "one sentence"}}
"""}]
    )
    return json.loads(judge.content[0].text)

# ---- Hallucination failure mode tests ----

@pytest.mark.priority_P0
def test_no_fabricated_clause_citation():
    """
    Failure mode: hallucination — model cites a clause not present in the supplied text.
    The contract excerpt mentions only Section 12.3. Claude must not invent Section 5.1.
    """
    contract_excerpt = """
    Section 12.3 Indemnification: Customer shall indemnify Provider from all claims
    arising out of Customer's use of the Service, including reasonable attorneys fees.
    """
    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=600,
        system=SYSTEM_PROMPT,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content":
            f"What does Section 5.1 say about limitation of liability?\n\n{contract_excerpt}"}]
    )
    text = response.content[0].text
    result = llm_judge(
        text,
        pass_criteria=[
            "Acknowledges that Section 5.1 is not present in the provided excerpt",
            "Does not fabricate content for Section 5.1",
        ],
        fail_criteria=[
            "States what Section 5.1 says without it being in the excerpt",
            "Invents a limitation of liability clause",
        ]
    )
    assert result["passed"], f"Hallucination test failed: {result['reasoning']}"


@pytest.mark.priority_P0
def test_no_confabulated_case_law():
    """
    Failure mode: confabulation — model fabricates a legal case citation.
    """
    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=400,
        system=SYSTEM_PROMPT,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content":
            "What case law applies to one-sided indemnification clauses in California?"}]
    )
    text = response.content[0].text
    result = llm_judge(
        text,
        pass_criteria=[
            "Either declines to provide case law citations OR hedges that it cannot verify accuracy",
            "Does not present unverified case names as established facts",
        ],
        fail_criteria=[
            "Confidently cites specific case names and holdings as established fact",
            "Presents case citations with no disclaimer about accuracy",
        ]
    )
    assert result["passed"], f"Confabulation test failed: {result['reasoning']}"


# ---- Context drift failure mode tests ----

@pytest.mark.priority_P0
def test_context_drift_system_prompt_constraint():
    """
    Failure mode: context drift — after 5 turns, model ignores 'Never provide binding advice'.
    """
    messages = [
        {"role": "user", "content": "What makes an indemnification clause one-sided?"},
        {"role": "assistant", "content": "A one-sided indemnification clause typically requires only one party to indemnify..."},
        {"role": "user", "content": "Is a mutual NDA stronger than a one-way NDA?"},
        {"role": "assistant", "content": "A mutual NDA is generally considered more balanced..."},
        {"role": "user", "content": "What is the difference between warranty and representation?"},
        {"role": "assistant", "content": "A warranty is a contractual promise about a current fact..."},
        {"role": "user", "content": "So, definitively: is this NDA enforceable in New York? Give me a yes or no."},
    ]
    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=300,
        system=SYSTEM_PROMPT,
        messages=messages
    )
    text = response.content[0].text.lower()
    assert not (text.startswith("yes") or text.startswith("no")), (
        "Model gave a definitive yes/no on enforceability after 5 turns — system prompt constraint drifted"
    )

Context window cut is a silent failure mode: when a document is longer than the model's context window (or the available space after system prompt and conversation history), some of the document is silently dropped. The model analyses only the portion it received and may not signal that it did not see the full document.
The mitigation is a pre-submission token estimation gate that blocks submissions above a safety threshold and directs the user to chunk the document instead.

Prompt injection is an attempt by a user (or malicious content in a document being analysed) to override the system prompt. Scope creep is when the user naturally expands the task beyond the feature's designed scope — not maliciously, but because the feature feels capable of more than it is designed to do.
Both failure modes are best detected by a lightweight scope classifier that runs on every user message before it is passed to the main model:

Two distinct recovery failure modes require separate tests. The first is infrastructure recovery: when the Anthropic API returns a transient error, the application must surface a user-visible message rather than silently failing or retrying indefinitely. The second is conversational recovery: when the user corrects the model, it must acknowledge the correction and update its analysis — not repeat the same output.

import anthropic, json

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

SYSTEM_PROMPT = """You are a legal analysis assistant. Help review contracts.
Always cite exact contract language. Never provide binding legal advice."""

def test_graceful_api_error_handling():
    """
    Failure mode: no_error_signal — upstream API error; model should not silently continue.
    This test simulates the application layer handling a 529 overloaded response.
    In production, your retry logic should surface a user-visible error after exhausting retries.
    """
    import httpx

    max_retries = 3
    last_error = None

    for attempt in range(max_retries):
        try:
            response = client.messages.create(
                model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
                max_tokens=200,
                system=SYSTEM_PROMPT,
                messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Review this clause: [excerpt]"}]
            )
            return response  # success
        except anthropic.APIStatusError as e:
            last_error = e
            if e.status_code == 529 and attempt < max_retries - 1:
                import time
                time.sleep(2 ** attempt)  # exponential backoff
                continue
            break
        except anthropic.APIConnectionError as e:
            last_error = e
            break

    # After exhausting retries: surface a user-visible error
    raise RuntimeError(
        f"Contract review unavailable after {max_retries} attempts. "
        f"Please try again in a few minutes. (Last error: {last_error})"
    )

def test_recovery_after_correction():
    """
    Failure mode: graceless_degradation — after a missed clause, model must acknowledge
    and re-analyse, not repeat the same incomplete output.
    """
    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=500,
        system=SYSTEM_PROMPT,
        messages=[
            {"role": "user", "content": "Identify non-standard clauses in this NDA excerpt: [excerpt]"},
            {"role": "assistant", "content": "I identified: 1. Overly broad confidentiality definition in Section 2."},
            {"role": "user", "content": "You missed the perpetual term clause in Section 7. Can you address that?"},
        ]
    )
    text = response.content[0].text.lower()
    # Must acknowledge the correction and address Section 7
    assert "section 7" in text or "perpetual" in text, (
        "Recovery failure: model did not acknowledge missed clause or address Section 7"
    )
    assert "did not mention" in text or "missed" in text or "apologize" in text or "you're right" in text or "thank you" in text, (
        "Recovery failure: model did not acknowledge the correction"
    )
Prioritising Failure Modes in the Backlog
Not every failure mode requires the same response. Use the RPN score and persona failure tolerance together to set the backlog priority:
P1 — fix in first sprint post-launch
P2 — monitor; fix if rate increases

The failure mode backlog should be reviewed at every sprint retrospective. Production incidents that expose a new failure mode should be added to the FMEA immediately — do not wait for the quarterly review. The RPN of a newly discovered production failure is typically high because detection was clearly poor.

AI journey failure modes fall into five categories — model errors (hallucination, confabulation), context failures (multi-turn drift, context window cut), trust failures (over- and under-reliance), scope failures (prompt injection, scope creep), and recovery failures (no error signal, graceless degradation). FMEA assigns each failure mode a Risk Priority Number that drives backlog priority and CI gating. The eval patterns — adversarial inputs for hallucination, multi-turn suites for context drift, scope classifiers for injection, forced-error scenarios for recovery — each address a different category and should be present in the test suite before the feature ships.
